frameworks.name: categorical perception
frameworks.one_line: Learned category boundaries sharpen cross-boundary perceptual discrimination

frameworks.name: Bezold-Brücke effect
frameworks.one_line: Hue shift with luminance accounting for error structure at the green-blue boundary

frameworks.name: labeling and verbal mediation in perceptual learning
frameworks.one_line: Verbal category labels mediate acquisition of perceptual distinctions

core_question: Does acquiring a novel colour category boundary pose different challenges for hue-defined versus lightness-defined distinctions?
key_assumptions: Category acquisition can be measured via post-training delayed same-different discrimination
Hue and lightness are dissociable dimensions of colour space
Trained boundaries within a single colour region (GREEN) isolate the learning effect
manipulations: Trained learners on hue-defined vs lightness-defined category boundary
Compared trained groups against untrained controls
Assessed delayed same-different discrimination for hue and lightness pairs
key_findings: Learning errors distributed non-uniformly consistent with the Bezold-Brücke effect
Only hue-learners showed post-training discrimination improvements
Hue-learners used 'green'/'blue' labels almost exclusively while lightness-learners used a wider label set most often 'light'/'dark'
Lightness-defined boundaries were less efficiently acquired than hue-defined boundaries
interpretations: Hue-category acquisition is more efficient than lightness-category acquisition
Verbal labels play an important role in colour category learning but do not conform to everyday colour naming
